children.name: Hirundo
children.description: Barn swallows — the most familiar swallows on Earth and one of the most widespread birds in the world. The barn swallow (ladusvala) is a summer visitor of immense cultural significance in Sweden: its arrival in late April or early May is celebrated as a defining moment of the Nordic year. Swallows have lived alongside humans for millennia, nesting in barns, stables, and buildings and feeding on the insects attracted to livestock — a mutualistic relationship that has made them simultaneously dependent on and beloved by farming communities. Swallows are aerial masters, capable of sustained flight speeds exceeding 70 km/h and catching all their food on the wing. They are long-distance migrants, wintering in sub-Saharan Africa and covering up to 10,000 km each way.

children.name: Hirundo rustica
children.description: The ladusvala — Sweden's most beloved summer visitor. Nests in barns and farm buildings; pair bonds are maintained across years with the same mate at the same nest. Long forked tail distinguishes it from all other Swedish swallows. Population declining across Europe due to loss of insect prey from agricultural intensification.

children.name: Hirundo lucida
children.description: A west and central African swallow closely resembling the barn swallow but with a smaller chestnut breast-band. It inhabits open woodland, savanna, and towns, often nesting on buildings. Previously treated as a subspecies of Hirundo rustica but now consistently recognized as a full species.

children.name: Hirundo angolensis
children.description: A small, rather plain swallow of central and southern Africa, distinguished by its dull chestnut throat and lack of a complete breast-band. It frequents open areas near rivers and towns, often perching on wires and rooftops. Its range is centered on Angola and the Democratic Republic of Congo.

children.name: Hirundo tahitica
children.description: A common and widespread swallow across South and Southeast Asia to the Pacific islands, often nesting under bridges and in culverts. It resembles a small barn swallow but has a shorter and less deeply forked tail. Multiple subspecies occur across its vast range, and it is resident year-round in tropical lowlands.

children.name: Hirundo neoxena
children.description: Australia's familiar swallow, equivalent in cultural role to Europe's barn swallow, and a common sight around farms, towns, and open country. It nests in structures ranging from sheds and carports to road culverts and even moving vehicles. The species has naturally colonized New Zealand since the 1950s, where it is now a breeding bird.

children.name: Hirundo nigrorufa
children.description: A distinctive, strikingly patterned swallow of the Angolan escarpment and adjacent Zambia, with entirely black upperparts and deep rufous underparts. It is an uncommon species of dry woodland and rocky hillsides at moderate altitude. Its biology and ecology remain poorly studied.

children.name: Hirundo smithii
children.description: One of the most elegant swallows, easily identified by its brilliant blue cap and the extraordinary wire-like extension of the outermost tail feathers, which trail several centimeters beyond the rest of the tail. It is a resident of rivers and lakes across Africa and South Asia, always found near water. The species was named in honor of the naturalist Andrew Smith.

children.name: Hirundo aethiopica
children.description: A small African swallow with a white forehead and pale chestnut throat, broadly similar to the barn swallow but resident rather than migratory. It inhabits open woodland and dry savanna across sub-Saharan Africa. The species frequently nests in culverts and bridges.

children.name: Hirundo nigrita
children.description: A striking, forest-associated swallow of west and central Africa, with glossy blue-black upperparts and a contrasting pure white throat and chest. Unlike most Hirundo swallows, it is tied to forest streams and rivers rather than open country. It is rarely observed far from water.

children.name: Hirundo dimidiata
children.description: A neat, blue-and-white swallow of southern and eastern Africa, notable for its entirely white underparts and the lack of a chestnut throat that characterizes many of its relatives. It frequents open woodland and savanna, breeding in rock crevices and occasionally in buildings. It is generally a year-round resident across its range.

children.name: Hirundo leucosoma
children.description: A distinctive west African swallow with strikingly patterned black-and-white wings and a white body contrasting with dark blue-black upperparts. It inhabits open woodland, savanna, and cultivated land from Senegal east to western Cameroon. The bold white wing patches visible in flight make it one of the most readily identified swallows in its range.

children.name: Hirundo megaensis
children.description: A critically endangered swallow known only from a tiny area around Mega in southern Ethiopia, making it one of the most restricted-range birds in Africa. It has an entirely white tail — unique among African hirundines — and glossy blue-black upperparts. The population is threatened by overgrazing, agriculture, and the extremely small extent of its habitat.

children.name: Cecropis
children.description: Striated and rufous swallows — a genus of Old World swallows split from Hirundo, characterized by streaked or rufous underparts and often prominent rufous rumps. They are found across Africa and Asia, with one species extending to Europe and another resident in Australia. Several are associated with rocky gorges and cliffs, while others nest in buildings and under bridges.

children.name: Cecropis daurica
children.description: A migratory swallow with a distinctive rufous rump and finely streaked underparts, breeding across southern Europe and Asia and wintering in Africa and South Asia. It builds enclosed mud nests with a tubular entrance attached to cliff faces, bridges, and buildings. A scarce but regular vagrant to northern Europe, occasionally recorded in Scandinavia.

children.name: Cecropis striolata
children.description: A large, boldly streaked swallow of South and Southeast Asia, with heavily striated underparts and a broad rufous rump. It inhabits open hilly country and rocky areas, building retort-shaped mud nests on cliff faces and buildings. Multiple subspecies vary substantially in the depth of streaking.

children.name: Cecropis badia
children.description: A relatively little-known swallow restricted to parts of Sumatra and Borneo, with deep rufous underparts and a rich chestnut rump. It was long treated as a subspecies of the striated swallow but is now regarded as a distinct species on the basis of its plumage, structure, and range. It inhabits forested hills and mountains.

children.name: Cecropis abyssinica
children.description: A widespread and conspicuous African swallow with a bright rufous cap, orange-rufous rump, and boldly streaked white underparts. It nests in enclosed mud structures under overhangs, roofs, and bridges across sub-Saharan Africa. The species is highly vocal and is a familiar presence in both rural and urban environments.

children.name: Cecropis senegalensis
children.description: The largest striped swallow in Africa, named for its habit of nesting in mosques and large buildings. It has a bold rufous rump and lightly streaked underparts. The species occupies open woodland and savanna across sub-Saharan Africa and often nests in tree cavities as well as structures.

children.name: Cecropis cucullata
children.description: A large, boldly marked swallow of southern Africa with a rufous cap, strongly streaked underparts, and an orange rump. It inhabits open and semi-open country from coastal grasslands to highland farmland. The species is a partial migrant, with some populations moving northward after breeding in the southern portion of its range.

children.name: Cecropis hyperythra
children.description: An endemic swallow of Sri Lanka and the Malabar coast of India, with striking rich rufous underparts and a dark blue crown and upperparts. It breeds in rocky gorges and wooded hills, building enclosed mud nests. Long treated as a subspecies of the red-rumped swallow, it is now consistently recognised as a full species.

children.name: Cecropis domicella
children.description: A poorly known swallow of coastal west Africa, closely related to the mosque swallow but distinguished by plumage differences and a more restricted range. It has rufous underparts with light streaking and nests in and around large buildings. This species requires further field study to clarify its distribution and behaviour.

children.name: Petrochelidon
children.description: Cliff swallows and allies — a genus of colonial swallows that build retort-shaped mud nests on cliff faces and man-made structures. The group spans North America, Africa, and Australia. The American cliff swallow is one of the most spectacular colonial birds in North America, with colonies sometimes numbering tens of thousands of nests under highway overpasses.

children.name: Petrochelidon pyrrhonota
children.description: One of North America's most colonial birds, building thousands of gourd-shaped mud nests on cliff faces and under bridges in densely packed aggregations. It migrates to South America for the winter, making a round trip of up to 12,000 km. The species is famous for its annual return to the Mission San Juan Capistrano in California.

children.name: Petrochelidon fulva
children.description: A colonial swallow that nests in caves, sinkholes, and culverts across the Caribbean and parts of the Americas. It has expanded significantly in range over recent decades by adopting man-made structures as nest sites. The species resembles the cliff swallow but differs in its paler forehead and less distinct breast-band.

children.name: Petrochelidon rufigula
children.description: A colonial cliff swallow of central and west Africa, distinguished by its rufous-red throat and forehead contrasting with dark upperparts. It builds mud nests in colonies on rocky outcrops, cliff faces, and sometimes large buildings. It is an uncommon and localized species within its range.

children.name: Petrochelidon preussi
children.description: A small cliff swallow of central and west Africa named after the German explorer and naturalist Paul Preuss. It nests colonially on rock faces and buildings and is generally uncommon within its fragmented range. Its taxonomy has been revised multiple times, and it was previously lumped with other African Petrochelidon.

children.name: Petrochelidon perdita
children.description: An enigmatic and very poorly known swallow described from a single specimen from Sudan and never definitively re-collected. Its specific epithet means 'lost', reflecting the mystery surrounding it. Whether it represents a genuine species, a hybrid, or an aberrant individual remains unresolved.

children.name: Petrochelidon nigricans
children.description: Australia's most familiar martin, a common aerial insectivore of open woodland and farmland that nests in tree hollows and old woodpecker holes, unlike the mud-nesting relatives in its genus. It is partially migratory, with southern populations moving north in winter. It often forages in large flocks and roosts communally in trees.

children.name: Petrochelidon ariel
children.description: A small, dainty martin of Australia recognized by its rufous cap and pure white underparts, building distinctive bottle-shaped mud nests in colonies under bridges, in culverts, and on cliff faces. It is nomadic, following rainfall and insect irruptions across the continent. The species name refers to its small, delicate appearance.

children.name: Petrochelidon fluvicola
children.description: A small, streaked swallow endemic to the Indian subcontinent, closely associated with rivers and lakes where it builds mud nests on structures above water. It has a distinctive streaked brown throat and breast that separates it from other Asian swallows. The species is a resident breeder across much of the Indian plains.

children.name: Petrochelidon frontalis
children.description: A colonial cliff swallow of the Himalayan foothills and hills of South and Southeast Asia, building enclosed mud nests on rocky cliff faces and under bridges. It is closely related to the streak-throated swallow and was long treated as its subspecies. The species has a more restricted distribution in the foothills of Nepal, Bangladesh, and adjacent Myanmar.

children.name: Petrochelidon rufocollaris
children.description: A small, colonial cliff swallow of western Ecuador and northwestern Peru, with a distinctive chestnut collar and forehead. It builds mud nests under bridges and on cliff faces in dry coastal valleys. The species has a restricted range in the Tumbesian region and is listed as near threatened due to habitat degradation.

children.name: Petrochelidon spilodera
children.description: A colonial cliff swallow endemic to southern Africa, building gourd-shaped mud nests on cliff faces, road bridges, and culverts across South Africa, Lesotho, and Eswatini. It is strongly migratory within Africa, wintering in central Africa. Large breeding colonies of thousands of nests can form under road bridges on the South African highveld.

children.name: Delichon
children.description: House martins — compact, blue-black and white swallows that build dome-shaped mud nests attached to cliff faces and — most famously — under the eaves of houses. The common house martin (hussvala) is a familiar summer visitor to Swedish towns and villages, and established colonies under roof eaves can be decades old. House martins collect mud for nest construction from puddles and stream banks, sometimes travelling considerable distances from their nesting site. They are more colonial than barn swallows, often building nests immediately adjacent to one another. Despite their name and intimate association with human buildings, relatively little is known about their winter quarters in sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Delichon urbicum
children.description: The hussvala — a familiar sight hawking insects above Swedish towns. White rump patch distinguishes it in flight from all other Swedish swallows. Builds mud nests under roof eaves; established colony sites may be used for generations. Declining in many European countries due to insect declines and renovation of old buildings.

children.name: Delichon dasypus
children.description: A close relative of the common house martin breeding across the Himalayas and mountains of East Asia, wintering in South and Southeast Asia. It is distinguished from its European relative by a slightly more heavily feathered tarsus and subtle plumage differences. Large flocks can be seen during migration, often mixing with other Delichon species.

children.name: Delichon nipalense
children.description: A high-altitude house martin of the Himalayas and mountains of Southeast Asia, closely tied to steep cliff faces and gorges where it builds large colonies of mud nests. It differs from related species in having a dark throat and a less-brilliant white rump. It is largely resident at lower elevations within its mountainous range in winter.

children.name: Riparia
children.description: Sand martins — the smallest and brownest of Sweden's swallows, colonial nesters in sandy riverbanks, sand quarries, and sea cliffs. Each pair excavates a tunnel 30–90 cm deep in bare sand, ending in a nesting chamber — a feat of engineering for a bird weighing just 13 grams. Sand martin colonies can number thousands of pairs and create dramatic, buzzing spectacles when the birds return to their colony after feeding. They are among the earliest summer migrants to arrive in Sweden in spring, and their presence at a colony site can be assessed instantly by the dark rings of nest holes peppering the sand face.

children.name: Riparia riparia
children.description: The backsvala — Sweden's earliest returning swallow. Colonial burrow-nester in riverbanks and sand quarries. One of the first migrants at Swedish spring seawatching points, often appearing in late March. Population fluctuates with Sahelian drought conditions in its African wintering grounds.

children.name: Riparia paludicola
children.description: A small, pale-throated martin widespread across Africa and parts of South Asia, nesting colonially in earthen banks along rivers and lakes. It lacks the brown breast-band of the sand martin and tends to have a more fluttering, less direct flight. Multiple subspecies differ in the extent of brown on the throat and upper breast.

children.name: Riparia congica
children.description: A little-studied riparian martin largely restricted to the Congo River system of central Africa, where it nests in sandy banks. It resembles the plain martin but is confined to the lowland Congo basin. Relatively few detailed ornithological records exist, and aspects of its ecology and movements are incompletely known.

children.name: Riparia cincta
children.description: The largest Riparia martin, found in open grassland and farmland across sub-Saharan Africa, distinctive for its wide dark breast-band contrasting with its white underparts. Unlike its relatives, it often nests in aardvark burrows and other pre-existing holes rather than excavating its own. It is a partial migrant in the southern part of its range.

children.name: Riparia diluta
children.description: A pale martin of central and South Asia, breeding along river systems and wintering in South Asia and Southeast Asia. It was previously treated as a subspecies of the sand martin but is now recognised as a separate species on the basis of its paler plumage, voice, and ecology. It nests in sandy river banks in a similar fashion to its relatives.

children.name: Riparia chinensis
children.description: A small martin of East and Southeast Asia, breeding along rivers and wintering in South and Southeast Asia, recently split from the plain martin. It inhabits open riparian and lacustrine habitats and excavates nest burrows in sandy banks like its relatives. The split is supported by differences in vocalisation and plumage tone.

children.name: Ptyonoprogne
children.description: Crag martins — robust, earth-coloured swallows of rocky mountains and gorges. The Eurasian crag martin nests on cliff faces and rocky outcrops, plastering a mud cup to vertical rock surfaces in locations completely inaccessible to terrestrial predators. They are resident year-round in southern Europe's mountain ranges, one of the few swallows that don't make long-distance migrations. In Sweden, crag martins are rare vagrants rather than breeding birds, occasionally seen along coastal cliffs in autumn. Their flight is more leisurely and gliding than other swallows, matching the thermal-soaring conditions of mountain environments.

children.name: Ptyonoprogne rupestris
children.description: A resident cliff-nesting swallow of southern European mountains. Rarely recorded in Sweden — an exciting vagrant. Plumage is a uniform brown unlike the contrasting patterns of other European swallows. White spots visible on spread tail in flight.

children.name: Ptyonoprogne obsoleta
children.description: A pale, sandy-brown crag martin of rocky deserts and semi-deserts from North Africa across the Middle East to Pakistan. It inhabits cliffs, gorges, and rocky outcrops in arid landscapes, often nesting in small colonies. Previously lumped with the Eurasian crag martin, it is now treated as a full species.

children.name: Ptyonoprogne fuligula
children.description: A widespread African crag martin of rocky country from the Saharan fringes south through East Africa to South Africa. It nests on cliff faces and rocky outcrops, building a mud cup in a sheltered crevice. Several subspecies vary in overall tone from pale sandy to dark brown, reflecting the diversity of rocky habitats across the continent.

children.name: Ptyonoprogne concolor
children.description: A uniformly dark brown crag martin of rocky hillsides and gorges in South and Southeast Asia. It is the darkest member of the genus, lacking the pale spots on the inner tail feathers present in other crag martins. Resident across much of its range, it nests in crevices and on cliff ledges.

children.name: Pseudhirundo
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the grey-rumped swallow of sub-Saharan Africa. The grey-rumped swallow is an unusual African hirundine that was long associated with Riparia but differs in its grey rump and preference for forest edge habitats. It builds its nest in burrows in banks or old woodpecker holes in trees.

children.name: Pseudhirundo griseopyga
children.description: A slender, long-tailed swallow of open woodland and forest clearings in sub-Saharan Africa, distinguished by its grey rump contrasting with dark upperparts. It nests in tree holes and burrows in earthen banks, often using the abandoned holes of other species. Long placed in Riparia, it is now treated as the sole member of its own genus.

children.name: Psalidoprocne
children.description: Saw-wings — a genus of African swallows named for the serrated outer primary feathers of the males, a unique structural feature among birds. They inhabit forest, woodland, and edge habitats across sub-Saharan Africa. Saw-wings are often associated with forest streams and ravines, and their flight style is more fluttering and bat-like than that of open-country swallows.

children.name: Psalidoprocne albiceps
children.description: A distinctive saw-wing of east and central Africa, immediately identified by the male's striking white head contrasting with otherwise dark plumage. It inhabits forest edge, river gorges, and rocky escarpments. The white head is largely absent in females, which are more uniformly dark.

children.name: Psalidoprocne antinorii
children.description: A uniformly brown saw-wing of northeast Africa, particularly Ethiopia and Eritrea, associated with forest edge and highland valleys. It is one of the less conspicuous saw-wings and can be difficult to distinguish from similar species in the field. Named after the Italian explorer Orazio Antinori, who collected extensively in northeast Africa.

children.name: Psalidoprocne fuliginosa
children.description: A sooty-black saw-wing of montane forest and highland bamboo zones in Cameroon, the island of Bioko, and adjacent central Africa. It is closely associated with cool, high-altitude forest streams and ravines. The species shows very little plumage variation between individuals.

children.name: Psalidoprocne holomelaena
children.description: A widespread, entirely black saw-wing found across east and southern Africa in montane forest and adjacent woodland. It is the most frequently observed saw-wing in much of eastern Africa. Both sexes are uniformly black, lacking the contrasting white head of some relatives.

children.name: Psalidoprocne nitens
children.description: A glossy blue-black saw-wing of west and central Africa, found in lowland and montane forest zones. Unlike most saw-wings, it lacks the distinctly forked tail typical of the genus, having instead a relatively square-cut tail. It inhabits the interior and edge of humid forest.

children.name: Psalidoprocne obscura
children.description: A dark, brownish-black saw-wing confined to forest and forest edge in West Africa from Sierra Leone to Ghana. It is a lowland forest specialist and is considered uncommon throughout its range. The species was described from the Fanti people's region of coastal Ghana.

children.name: Psalidoprocne orientalis
children.description: A dark saw-wing of east African montane forests from Ethiopia south to Malawi and Mozambique. It is closely related to the black saw-wing and was previously treated as conspecific with it. The eastern saw-wing tends to favour highland forest and forest edge at elevations above 1,500 m.

children.name: Psalidoprocne petiti
children.description: A glossy black saw-wing of humid forests and forest edge in central Africa, found from Cameroon to Uganda and the Congo Basin. It was named after the French naturalist Eugène Petit. The species inhabits dense forest, making it less frequently encountered than saw-wings of more open habitats.

children.name: Psalidoprocne pristoptera
children.description: The most widespread saw-wing in Africa, found in open woodland, grassland, and forest edge from west Africa across the continent to Ethiopia and south to South Africa. It is mostly glossy blue-black with varying amounts of white on the underparts depending on subspecies. The name pristoptera refers to the distinctive saw-like outer primary feather.

children.name: Psalidoprocne oleaginea
children.description: A glossy, oil-green saw-wing of the rainforest zone of central Africa, found in the Congo Basin and Cameroon. It inhabits primary and tall secondary forest, especially near rivers and forest streams. The species is locally uncommon and less frequently recorded than other central African saw-wings.

children.name: Phedina
children.description: A small genus of African martins with streaked underparts, found in Madagascar and central Africa. They inhabit open country near water and forest clearings, and are among the less well-studied hirundines. The Mascarene martin is a migratory species that moves between breeding and wintering areas within the Indian Ocean region.

children.name: Phedina borbonica
children.description: A streaked, brown martin of Madagascar and the Mascarene Islands, with heavily streaked underparts that distinguish it from most other African hirundines. It is a partial migrant, with the Madagascan population wintering in east Africa. The species inhabits open country and forest margins near rivers and lakes.

children.name: Phedina brazzae
children.description: A little-known, streaked martin confined to the Congo River basin in central Africa, named after the French-Italian explorer Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza. It is associated with the Congo River and its tributaries, where it flies low over the water surface. This species remains one of the least-studied of all African hirundines.

children.name: Progne
children.description: Purple martins and their allies — the largest swallows in the Americas, with glossy blue-black plumage in males and more dusky or brown females. Progne martins are strongly associated with human-provided nest boxes and gourds in North America, where the purple martin has become almost entirely dependent on man-made nest structures east of the Rockies. South American species tend to inhabit open savanna and forest edge, often nesting in palm cavities.

children.name: Progne subis
children.description: North America's largest swallow, the male being entirely glossy blue-purple. East of the Rocky Mountains, the species is almost entirely dependent on nest boxes and gourds provided by a devoted community of human 'landlords'. Migrates to South America and has one of the most studied migration systems of any North American bird.

children.name: Progne cryptoleuca
children.description: A Caribbean martin endemic as a breeding bird to Cuba, with a concealed white patch on the lower belly in the male that gives it its name. It migrates to South America in winter, where its wintering grounds are not well-documented. Previously treated as a subspecies of the purple martin, it is now regarded as a distinct species.

children.name: Progne dominicensis
children.description: A martin breeding across the Caribbean islands from Jamaica and Hispaniola to the Lesser Antilles, wintering in South America. The male has white underparts below the breast contrasting with the glossy blue-black hood. It nests in cavities in cliffs and buildings and is common in many Caribbean islands.

children.name: Progne elegans
children.description: A large, entirely glossy blue-black martin of southern South America, breeding in Argentina, Bolivia, and adjacent countries and migrating north to Amazonia in winter. The male is indistinguishable from the purple martin in the field, requiring range to establish identity. It nests in rock crevices and in buildings in urban areas.

children.name: Progne chalybea
children.description: A widespread martin from Mexico south through Central and South America to northern Argentina, with the male having glossy blue-black upperparts and grey-white underparts. It nests in buildings, under bridges, and in palm cavities. This species is commonly seen in urban settings and is one of the most familiar swallows in South America.

children.name: Progne modesta
children.description: A small, dark martin endemic to the Galapagos Islands, the only swallow resident in the archipelago. Both sexes are largely blackish, the male entirely so. It is a relatively common bird across the islands, nesting in crevices in rocky lava flows and in sea cliffs.

children.name: Progne murphyi
children.description: A coastal martin endemic to Peru, restricted to the Pacific coastal lowlands and adjacent valleys. Named after the American ornithologist Robert Cushman Murphy, it was long treated as a subspecies of the southern martin. It inhabits sea cliffs, river valleys, and open semi-arid coastal scrub.

children.name: Progne tapera
children.description: The only Progne martin with brown upperparts and a bold brown breast-band on white underparts, making it readily identifiable. It breeds from Central America to central South America, wintering in Amazonia. The species uses old burrows of the rufous hornero and other hole-nesters rather than building its own nest.

children.name: Progne sinaloae
children.description: A recently described martin endemic to the Pacific slope of western Mexico, breeding in the foothills of Sinaloa and adjacent states before migrating to South America. Named for the Mexican state of Sinaloa where it was first recognized as a distinct species. It resembles the grey-breasted martin but has a darker breast-band and different vocalizations.

children.name: Tachycineta
children.description: A New World genus of iridescent green, blue, or violet swallows, including the tree swallow — one of the most studied birds in the world due to its enthusiastic adoption of nest boxes. Tachycineta swallows range from Alaska to Tierra del Fuego, with several island endemics in the Caribbean. Their tree swallow populations have served as a model for understanding the effects of climate change on avian breeding phenology.

children.name: Tachycineta bicolor
children.description: One of the most intensively studied birds in the world, with nest-box populations across North America yielding decades of data on breeding ecology, reproductive success, and climate change responses. The male is vivid iridescent green above and white below. Tree swallows are one of the first migrants to return to northern breeding grounds in spring.

children.name: Tachycineta thalassina
children.description: A stunning swallow of western North America, with males showing vivid violet-green iridescent upperparts and extensive white on the face and rump sides. It breeds from Alaska south to Mexico in open forests and nest boxes, wintering in Central America. The species forages at greater heights than most swallows.

children.name: Tachycineta cyaneoviridis
children.description: An endangered swallow endemic to the pine forests of the northern Bahamas, one of the most restricted-range birds in the Caribbean. It nests in cavities in Caribbean pine, a habitat increasingly threatened by logging and development. The global population is estimated at fewer than 2,000 individuals.

children.name: Tachycineta stolzmanni
children.description: A little-known swallow of arid coastal northwestern Peru and southwestern Ecuador, named after the Polish naturalist Jan Sztolcman. It inhabits dry tropical forest and scrub near the Pacific coast and is rarely observed outside its restricted range. Little is known of its nesting biology.

children.name: Tachycineta albiventer
children.description: A striking Amazonian swallow instantly identified by its broad white wingbars and white rump, unlike any other swallow in its range. It is common along rivers and lakes throughout Amazonia and the Orinoco basin. The species nests in cavities in dead snags over water and is often observed perching on branches overhanging rivers.

children.name: Tachycineta meyeni
children.description: A migratory swallow breeding in open country in Patagonia and the Falkland Islands and wintering northward to Brazil. It closely resembles the tree swallow in plumage but has white extending onto the face and cheeks. The species named in honour of the German naturalist Franz Julius Ferdinand Meyen.

children.name: Tachycineta leucorrhoa
children.description: A common swallow of open grassy habitats, farmland, and wetlands in southern South America, identified by its white rump and forehead patch. It breeds from Brazil and Bolivia south to Argentina and Uruguay, moving north in the austral winter. It frequently nests in cavities in buildings and fence posts.

children.name: Tachycineta euchrysea
children.description: A spectacular Caribbean swallow, with males showing brilliant golden-green iridescent upperparts — among the most vivid plumage of any hirundine. It has two subspecies: one in Jamaica and one in Hispaniola, the latter now feared extinct or nearly so. The Jamaican population is considered vulnerable due to habitat loss.

children.name: Tachycineta albilinea
children.description: A small, white-rumped swallow closely tied to mangroves, rivers, and coastal waterways from Mexico south through Central America to Panama and northwest Peru. It is identified by its white rump and the white line above the eye. The species nests in cavities in dead trees over or near water.

children.name: Stelgidopteryx
children.description: Rough-winged swallows — a New World genus of two species named for the tiny hook-like serrations on the outer primary feathers of the males. They are dull brown swallows without the iridescent plumage of many relatives, but are widespread and common in a variety of open habitats throughout the Americas. They nest in burrows and crevices in earth banks, often using old kingfisher holes.

children.name: Stelgidopteryx serripennis
children.description: A common but unassuming brown swallow of North and Central America, often nesting in drainage pipes and eroded stream banks. It is a widespread breeder across most of the United States and Canada in summer. Winters in Central America. Named for the small spines on the outer primary feathers, visible only in the hand.

children.name: Stelgidopteryx ruficollis
children.description: A widespread rough-winged swallow of Central and South America, distinguished from its northern counterpart by the tawny or rufous wash on the throat and upper breast. It inhabits open country near water, forest edges, and towns throughout its range. Multiple subspecies vary in the depth of the rufous coloration.

children.name: Cheramoeca
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the white-backed swallow, one of Australia's most distinctively patterned hirundines. The genus is characterised by a unique combination of features including a deeply forked tail, white back, and pied plumage. The white-backed swallow occupies arid and semi-arid Australia, nesting in burrows it excavates in sandy soil.

children.name: Cheramoeca leucosterna
children.description: A strikingly patterned Australian swallow with a pure white back and cap contrasting with dark wings, rump, and tail. It inhabits open arid and semi-arid country throughout interior Australia and excavates its nest burrow in flat sandy ground rather than in banks. The species is nomadic, following rainfall patterns across the dry interior.

children.name: Alopochelidon
children.description: A monotypic genus comprising the tawny-headed swallow of South America. The genus has sometimes been placed within Stelgidopteryx but morphological and molecular data support its recognition as a distinct genus. The tawny-headed swallow is a bird of open woodland and savanna in central South America.

children.name: Alopochelidon fucata
children.description: A small, dull-plumaged swallow of open savanna and woodland edges in central South America, from southeastern Brazil to Bolivia and northern Argentina. It is identified by its tawny or rufous-buff crown and nape. The species nests in burrows in earthen banks and is somewhat nomadic in the non-breeding season.

children.name: Atticora
children.description: A genus of two elegant Amazonian swallows with distinctive banded or jet-black plumage. Atticora swallows are associated with major Amazonian rivers and their tributaries. They nest colonially in sandy riverbanks and are among the more distinctive hirundines of the Amazon basin.

children.name: Atticora fasciata
children.description: A striking, glossy black swallow of Amazonian rivers, identified by a broad white band across the breast and white on the lower belly. It is closely tied to large rivers and forages low over water surface in fast, direct flight. The species nests colonially in burrows in sandy riverbanks.

children.name: Atticora melanoleuca
children.description: A glossy black swallow with white underparts and a bold black breast-band, confined to rocky stretches of large Amazonian rivers where it nests in crevices among rocks. It is particularly associated with rapids and waterfalls, and is rarely found away from these specialized habitats. The species forages low over the churning water between rocks.

children.name: Atticora andecola
children.description: A high-altitude swallow of the open Andean puna, found from Peru south to northwestern Argentina and Chile at elevations typically above 3,500 m. It is the least typical Atticora, with plain brown upperparts and pale underparts, and was formerly placed in its own genus Haplochelidon. The species nests in burrows in earthen banks and is locally migratory in the non-breeding season.

children.name: Neochelidon
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the white-thighed swallow of northern South America, sometimes placed in Atticora. It is a small, dark swallow of forest edge and open country in lowland Amazonia and adjacent areas, identified by its white thigh tufts visible in flight.

children.name: Neochelidon tibialis
children.description: A small, dull brownish-black swallow of lowland forest edge and clearings in Amazonia and Trinidad, with distinctive white tufts on the thighs that are visible in the hand. It forages at canopy level over forest and at forest edge, unlike most swallows that forage in open country. The species nests in termitaria and tree holes.

children.name: Pygochelidon
children.description: A genus of two Andean and southern South American swallows formerly placed in Notiochelidon. They are small, blue-black swallows of open Andean habitats and Patagonian grasslands, often nesting in towns and villages at high altitude.

children.name: Pygochelidon cyanoleuca
children.description: One of the most familiar and abundant swallows in South America, found from lowland cities to Andean towns above 4,000 m. The species is glossy blue-black above and white below with black undertail coverts. Northern populations are resident while southern ones migrate to warmer latitudes in the austral winter.

children.name: Pygochelidon patagonica
children.description: A recently separated species, formerly treated as the migratory subspecies of the blue-and-white swallow, breeding in Patagonia and Tierra del Fuego. It is a long-distance austral migrant, wintering as far north as Bolivia and Brazil. The species inhabits open Patagonian steppe and grassland near water.

children.name: Orochelidon
children.description: Andean martins — a genus of two high-altitude swallows of the Andes formerly placed in Notiochelidon. They inhabit open Andean puna and grassland, often at elevations above 3,000 m. Both are dark swallows with pale underparts and are typical aerial feeders of the open Andean sky.

children.name: Orochelidon murina
children.description: A dark swallow of high Andean grasslands and puna from Venezuela south to Bolivia, distinguished by its brownish underparts contrasting with glossy blue-black upperparts. It nests in crevices in rocky outcrops and earthen banks at high altitude. Often observed in flocks foraging over open Andean grassland.

children.name: Orochelidon flavipes
children.description: A restricted-range swallow of montane forest and forest edge in Peru and Bolivia, with yellowish-flesh feet and a contrasting dark cap. It inhabits the humid eastern Andes at mid to high elevation, and is less tied to open puna than the brown-bellied swallow. Relatively little is known about its breeding biology.

children.name: Pseudochelidon
children.description: River martins — a primitive genus of two highly distinctive swallows placed in their own subfamily, Pseudochelidoninae, owing to their aberrant morphology: they have large eyes, heavy bills, stout legs, and rounded wings unlike any other hirundine. The African river martin breeds on sandbars in the Congo River, while the white-eyed river martin was last confirmed on its Thai wintering grounds in the 1970s and may be extinct.

children.name: Pseudochelidon eurystomina
children.description: A large, powerful swallow of the Congo River in central Africa, nesting colonially in burrows in sandy islands and sandbars exposed during the dry season. Its morphology — heavy bill, large eyes, stout legs — is so different from other swallows that it is placed in its own subfamily. Known to roost in vast flocks in reed beds of the Congo, its wintering areas are still incompletely mapped.

children.name: Pseudochelidon sirintarae
children.description: One of the most enigmatic birds in the world, known from a handful of specimens collected at Boraphet lake in central Thailand in 1968 and not reliably recorded since. Named in honour of Princess Maha Chakri Sirindhorn of Thailand. It is listed as critically endangered or possibly extinct; targeted searches have failed to locate it and its breeding grounds have never been found.
